title: Forms in Triplicate
description: One notice on the community center board reads: CITIZEN COMPLAINT RESOLUTION — ESTIMATED WAIT TIME 14-18 MONTHS. Forty-seven unresolved complaints sit in the HOA's backlog. Seven are flagged red. None have ever been processed.

offer.text: The notice is laminated, level, and centered. CITIZEN COMPLAINT RESOLUTION: ESTIMATED WAIT TIME 14-18 MONTHS.

offer.speaker: Rogue
offer.text: That's not a wait time. That's a system designed to not resolve anything. You don't laminate a number you intend to improve.

accept.speaker: Mage
accept.text: Public records are requestable. Form 22-B. We'll need proof of residency — or whatever this neighborhood accepts as proof of being a person.

decline.text: The board stays where it is. The wait time does not change. The wait time has never changed. That is arguably the point of laminating it.

in_progress.speaker: Mage
in_progress.text: Forty-seven complaints. Seven flagged red, auto-escalated to a review queue that has never contained an item. The system is a loop wearing a filing cabinet.

ready_to_turn_in.speaker: Mail Carrier
ready_to_turn_in.text: You processed them? All seven? Let me see the receipt stamps. I've never actually seen one before. I want to know what they look like.

complete.speaker: Mail Carrier
complete.text: Official public record. Seven complaints, fourteen to eighteen months early. The HOA is going to be very polite about this and then very quiet about this.

complete.speaker: Mail Carrier
complete.text: The one from Phil — the old man's complaint. 'Something is wrong with the counting.' I hand-delivered that one myself, years ago. I always wondered where it went. Now I know: nowhere. It went nowhere on purpose.

complete.speaker: Mail Carrier
complete.text: Show him it's filed, if you're ever back that way. I think it would matter to him. Things that are written down matter to him.

objectives.description: Read the laminated notice on the bulletin board.
Obtain the complaint backlog via Form 22-B.
Get the seven flagged complaints processed — somehow.
Show the mail carrier the receipt stamps.

unlocks_dialogue.note: W1 Phil, shown his own filed complaint: 'I wrote this. I don't remember writing this. But I wrote this.' Folds it into his pocket. 'Good. If I wrote it down, then it happened.'
Coordinator's final-confrontation opening gains a line if complete: 'You filed the complaints. I see. I'd hoped the backlog would absorb them. It usually does.'

cross_world_connection: W4: the monitors briefly display Phil's complaint as an archived document — 'REVIEW REQUIRED. STATUS: PERPETUALLY PENDING.' The foreman: 'That complaint's been in the system for years. It was escalated before the factory was built. How is that possible?'
_wiring_notes: Giver is an interactable (bulletin board), tally-wall pattern — examine = offer, emits quest_world2_forms_in_triplicate_notice_read. Step 2 emitter quest_world2_forms_in_triplicate_backlog_obtained: front-desk interact gated on a credential — visitor credential from the mail carrier counts (shared with the Orrery-chain errand if that lands later; grant it here standalone via a brief front-desk sub-dialogue if not). Step 3 emitter quest_world2_forms_in_triplicate_complaints_processed is MULTI-PATH, any one: (a) Bard-lead composes the formal complaint narrative (dialogue interact), (b) Rogue-lead finds and enters the processing code directly, (c) anyone: bring it to the mail carrier who files third-party as a federal employee. The seven flagged complaints as readable content: 3× rearranging strip mall, 2× missing children, 1× the monitor visible through the Coordinator's window, 1× Phil the Lost: 'I keep seeing this village. Different every time. Same people. Something is wrong with the counting.' Phil's is the load-bearing one — it cross-references W1 and seeds the counting motif.
